title: Low-Cost Robotic Arm
quick_description: Affordable, open-source robotic arm for hands-on learning and experimentation

overview.title: Overview
overview.text: The Low-Cost Robotic Arm was inspired by an earlier LEGO arm I built. In talking with Dr. Briana Bouchard, we reflected on how expensive the LEGO system was and how unintuitive its programming could be. That conversation sparked the idea of creating a more affordable and accessible alternative, and we teamed up to make it feasible.

items.value:
Designing the Gripper was one of the most challenging parts of the project. Because it sits at the very end of the arm, the weight was a critical constraint. A smaller SG90 servo was chosen, which provided enough torque to handle small objects.

Several concepts were tested before reaching the final version. Each option revealed new trade-offs and informed the next iteration. The process highlighted the constraints shape design choices. Small refinements ultimately made the difference between a fragile prototype and a dependable solution.

A big part of the project was writing the control code for the arm. I implemented inverse kinematics using trigenometry to simplify IK. This allowed the arm be move to a specific Cartesian position. A simple WASD interface for direct control was also added. This was my first time combining mechanical design with real-time programming, which gave me a deeper understanding of how hardware and software work together.

value: Build a fully functional 4-DOF robotic arm using low-cost, 3D-printed parts and off-the-shelf electronics.
Design a lightweight custom gripper that reliably handles small objects.
Implement key robotics concepts such as kinematics, control systems, and programming for under $30.

reflection.title: Reflection
reflection.text: This project taught me how constraints like weight, torque, and printability shape design choices. I learned the value of rapid iteration, designing for manufacturability, and staying persistent when early versions failed. It was also my first time leading a design project from concept to reality, learning new skills on the fly and turning an idea into a working system. Most importantly, it showed me how affordable tools can make robotics education more accessible and inspiring.

content.value: Purpose: the purpose of the project is ...Actuaid is an assistive device designed to help patients with limited mobility perform rehabilitation exercises. The device uses a combination of sensors and actuators to provide feedback and assistance during exercises, making it easier for patients to complete their rehabilitation routines. The device is designed to be user-friendly and easy to use, with a simple interface that allows patients to customize their exercise routines. The device is also designed to be portable, allowing patients to take it with them wherever they go.

The device worked with a glove that had flex sensors on the thumb. On the box itself, you could set the pressure and speed of the pumps. The pumps would inflate the pneumatic actuator to help with rehabilitation.

title: PID Controlled Car
quick_description: PID-controlled car with web interface and multi-car network communication.

content.value: The multi-car network communication allows for a web interface allows users to monitor and control the car remotely, while the network communication enables multiple cars to coordinate their movements and avoid falling off the ramp. The car is built using a combination of 3D-printed and laser-cut components, with a focus on looking good and ease of assembly.

A key part of this project was building the H-bridge motor driver circuit. I explored this process independently, before it was covered in class, and figured out how to wire and test the transistors to safely switch motor directions. Once I validated the design on a breadboard, I transferred the circuit into a PCB layout and integrated it into the car. This early work helped my team move faster when it came time to apply it to the full system.

title: Collaborative Robotics Projects (Tufts Simple Robotics)
quick_description: Introduction to robotics through LEGO — exploring gears, sensors, and creative mechanisms.

content.value: Walking Mechanism – Inspired by duck-like motion, creating a LEGO walker that 'falls forward' to walk.
LEGO Spirograph – Integrated my tablet on a LEGO mechanism to draw intricate designs (only one in the class to attempt this).
LEGO 2D Printer – Integrated my tablet on an XY-axis LEGO mechanism to draw squares (only one in the class to attempt this).
PS4-Controlled LEGO Car – Connected a LEGO car to a PS4 controller and tablet. Later expanded this into a LEGO snake showcased at a Haunted House.
Final Project: Repurposed WALL-E Set – Added motors and PS4 controller support for full interactive control.

title: Metal Casting Project: Pewter Soccer Ball
quick_description: Designed and cast a pewter soccer ball trophy through multiple mold-making iterations.

content.value: For our ME10 final project, my team and I set out to recreate the Ballon d’Or trophy as a pewter soccer ball. The project became an exploration of casting methods, iteration, and creative problem solving.

We began by attempting to cast a half-sphere of the ball directly, but quickly learned that the plastic mold would melt under molten pewter. To overcome this, I suggested vacuum forming the ball to create a wax replica, which could then be packed in sand and replaced with metal during the pour.

content.caption: Ball mold experiment before transitioning to Wax

content.value: The wax casting approach worked in theory but presented new issues with removing the wax cleanly and creating a hollow interior. After several rounds of testing, we switched to making a hard silicone base mold. This allowed us to pour pewter safely and achieve a consistent final shape.

content.caption: Wax mold experiment before transitioning to silicone
Final pewter casting mounted on CNC-routed wooden base

content.value: The final product combined creativity with practical problem solving. This project taught me not only the technical aspects of casting, but also how to pivot and refine designs through hands-on experimentation.

content.caption: Final pewter casting mounted on CNC-routed wooden base

title: CAD & Manufacturing Foundations
quick_description: Introductory projects in CAD modeling and manufacturing.

content.value: Developed foundational skills in CAD modeling and manufacturing workflows. Modeled real-world objects such as a soccer ball, company logo, and an orange peel with attention to tolerances. Documented design-to-production processes through detailed imagery and notes.

title: Mechanisms & Motion Design
quick_description: Exploring motion and mechanisms through CAD.

content.value: Designed and modeled dynamic mechanical assemblies with gears and linkages. Created interactive tracks inspired by Mario Kart, including Stair Road, Rainbow Road, and Mushroom Kingdom. Analyzed and simulated motion to understand mechanical behavior and system interaction.

title: Product CAD & Accessibility Projects
quick_description: Designing consumer products with accessibility in mind.

content.value: Modeled and prototyped components for consumer products such as a hair dryer and electric drill. Collaborated on an assistive wheelchair project, emphasizing accessibility and user-centered design. Documented processes for design iteration, testing, and manufacturing readiness.

title: Robovend Senior Design Project
quick_description: Senior design project focused on robotic automation.

content.value: Developed a robotic arm for automated vending as part of a team-based senior design project. Led mechanical design iterations and conducted performance testing for precision and reliability. Compiled full documentation and presented the project, highlighting collaboration and engineering problem-solving.

title: CAD Portfolio
quick_description: A collection of projects showcasing Computer-Aided Design skills and techniques.

content.title: Overview
content.text: This portfolio collection highlights my proficiency in Computer-Aided Design (CAD) through various projects. It demonstrates my ability to translate conceptual ideas into detailed 3D models, perform complex assemblies, and generate technical drawings. The projects showcase my learning and application of industry-standard CAD software and design principles.

content.value: This class was my introduction to CAD using SolidWorks. I practiced creating parts, assemblies, and realistic models to build a foundation in digital design.

content.title: LEGO Brick
content.description: Replicated an existing part with accurate proportions to strengthen parametric modeling skills.

content.title: Mounting Bracket
content.description: Learned precision dimensioning and constraints for mechanical parts.

content.title: Citrus Press Assembly
content.description: Practiced combining multiple parts into a functional assembly with moving components.

content.title: Coffee Mug
content.description: Practiced surface modeling, lofts, and fillets to recreate a common object.

content.title: Pumpkin
content.description: Used advanced features such as revolves and patterns to create a complex organic shape.
